title: I spend 90% of my time on AX. Here’s why.
author: Thomas Hoffmann
text:
I spend 90% of my time on AX. Here’s why.
Here’s something I’ve experienced over the past year working with AI Agents to build products:
Agents don’t make mistakes because they’re stupid. They make mistakes because they’re smart.
The outcome might not be what you wanted. But given the information it had, the agent made the correct decision. And when your codebase is full of duplication, inconsistent patterns and multiple ways of doing the same thing, “smart” decisions start going sideways, fast.
I’ve been a software engineer for over 20 years. For the past year, I’ve been working in what I call augmented engineering — humans and AI agents working together as a team. This is not vibe coding. Not agents replacing developers. Not developers using agents as glorified auto-complete. A genuine collaboration where I set direction, provide context and take care of operational concerns such as testing and quality, observability, security and so forth. AI agents execute, iterate and deliver alongside me. I even chat with my AI agents throughout the day, the same way I would with a team of software engineers.
That experience has completely reframed how I think about three things that determine whether software succeeds or fails:
UX (User Experience) — how your users experience your product
DX (Developer Experience) — how your developers experience building it
AX (Agent Experience) — how AI agents experience working in your codebase, your tooling, your infrastructure
That last one is new. Matt Biilmann, CEO of Netlify, introduced the term AX early last year. Jim Nielsen pushed back, arguing UX should remain the top priority.
I understand that view, but I see it differently.
It’s not a priority ranking… It’s a supply chain.
AX feeds DX feeds UX. If the foundation is broken, everything above it degrades — no matter how loudly you say you “prioritise the user.”
Every developer already knows this dynamic. Poor DX doesn’t just slow delivery — it changes how people think and work — It affects your overall job satisfaction.
In the worst case, you become complacent. Accept the system as-is.
In the best case, you fight cycle after cycle for time to address tech debt instead of shipping features. “Rewrite” becomes a dirty word. Migration limbo seems never-ending.
That doesn’t produce innovation and productivity. It produces fatigue.
Now apply that same logic one layer deeper.
In a world where more and more software is being build by AI agents, AX is everything.
Working in augmented engineering feels a bit like being a team lead — only now my team is made up of AI agents who work a little bit differently. I find myself trying to focus on a feature and bing pulled back into agent tooling because the agents aren’t delivering the results I need.
Here’s what I’ve had to accept: that’s not their mess. It’s mine.
Agents don’t browse, skim or improvise. They need clear signals. Consistent patterns. One obvious way to do something. When your codebase has grown organically — full of workarounds, competing conventions and tribal knowledge that only lives in people’s heads — agents don’t muddle through the way a developer might. They pick the wrong path confidently and keep going.
As I’ve improved the tooling and infrastructure for my agents, the quality of their work has improved dramatically. I’m spending roughly 90% of my time on AX right now to get the UX I’m looking for and the difference is measurable.
So here’s my mental model.
Forget the linear ranking. Think of one of those 4-way see-saws from a playground — the ones shaped like a “+” that kids love. Instead of kids on each end, you’ve got AX, DX, UX, and “feature delivery”.
The goal isn’t “perfect balance.” Everything is a moving target. If everyone is too heavy or too light, nothing moves. You just want to make sure nobody’s hitting the ground when they bounce down, and nobody’s flying off when they bounce up.
